author: @thomashverring
text:
I would love to have a 3D printer, but I can't afford it. A friend of mine has said I can loan his old one, so I hope to do that. But that's from when you had to be an engineer to make it work, so that's the downside. But I'm quite excited to loan it :^)

On the other point you made. I really want a 3D printer to make all kinds of stuff (models for board game design, parts for machine designs, etc.), but that doesn't mean I lose touch with materials, because I'm a hand tool woodworker as well, and that's still going to be traditional hand tools, because that's part of the joy of it. Different tasks, different tools—whatever makes you happy :^)

author: @MakerBees333
text: I have pre-ordered the Max myself. I have had many troubles with affordable printers in the past 2 years. While I have been hard on printer never unreasonable and I always keep up maintenance.  I think about 2 years from now many reliable options will be more affordable but I have extended my faith again hoping I can get at least a year out of the Max. My Sovol SV05 lasted 3mo. My Kobra needed big repairs after 4months, Weedo lasted a year then tried to catch fire…. 🤞here is for hoping.

author: @jamest.5001
text: From about  4:00+/-, to near the end ,  I feel you are talking to me! 😮 Yeah I have a slight fear about 3D printing,  but I'd like to print parts to cast them in iron or aluminum to build a lathe and a mill,  and possibly a oil burning furnace , to build a small foundry and machine shop,  using scrap metal and parts from salvage, alloy rims  from cars are excellent quality, and adding a piston or two to get more silicon ,  plus I want to build a modern version of a 1920's diesel engine, opposed twin , for  stability.  Maybe a 2, cycle version, using a second crank and piston to scavenger or handle the intake stroke, forcing the remaining exhaust out,  filling the cylinder with fresh air,  a 2-2.5 :1 stroke to bore ratio,  4" bore 10" stroke,   sorry to ramble..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't mind rambling mate I think that is awesome stuff - but I can see how a 3d printer would help - model making patterns for casting - all kinds of things really - most of the time the parts I make aren't finished parts they are just part of my process if I want finished parts I would - like you  - do more machining - but it cetainly helps with models, parts, patterns and process

author: @Ireallymissmymind
text:
I am with you all the way Robert. You have had a sort of butterfly effect on this small patch of the Highlands. 
I have two workshops full (rather too full) of stock, tools and machines collected over the past 65 years. I ordered my Neptune 3 for exactly the same reasons as yours the night I watched your videos about it and the Ugrinsky turbine. It arrived in January - that was the easy bit. Turns out we needed a third workshop that was heated, easily cleaned and mouseproof. 
One life-laundry, some arguments, a room clearance and redecoration and an awful lot of Amazon deliveries later we have a studio in the house which, we discover, needs a dodgy patio door replaced with something better, which will happen this summer - at some expense. - So, many thanks to you and yes, the Neptune 3 Pro is a great machine at a great price, I just hadn't thought the rest of it all through.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lololol - once the first domino falls eh mate lol

author: @ImSaari
text: What a tool!

author: @ET_AYY_LMAO
text:
3D Printers have really improved the last decade. When I got into it around 2013 it was either prohibitively expensive, or you had to build one yourself, the slicer software was primitive, not even a real preview, SD cards were a thing but most people just used a serial connection to a PC and host software. 
Its so nice to see that you don't have to dedicate yourself to 3D printing in the same way anymore, now it is - As you say - A tool, it was before, but now it is literally for everyone. As you say, 4 screws and you are going - Back in the RepRap days, you had to source parts, find someone to 3D print parts for you, configure custom firmware, solder stuff etc.

Eventually it will need a little maintenance, but I am sure you will handle that no problem. The worst thing about this particular style of printers is actually NOT using it, as it can deform the rollers on the axis over time.
My old RepRap ran for 10years and still works, but it has been retired.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am with you mate - they have come along way

author: @vigilante004
text: Mr. Smith, My name is Eric Marion. I have always been hyper curious about most things. I am constantly creative and mostly uneducated in the formal sense. Being 59 yrs. old and having been “educated” in the American school system, I learned after 1st grade in 1969 that I would have to rely on my own means to learn stuff. Anyway, that being said……my next question might seem kind of pointless. I have been trying to print out the flat wind generator now for the past 3 days. I won’t stop until I succeed. Once I do have a working model, I was thinking that I would use its power output to power a very small computer cooling fan. If that is possible, I’d turn that fan to point at the wind generator, thus creating perpetual motion. Should I worry about the M.I.B. (Men in Black) visiting me if that experiment should work? Or is there some sort of principle about output can never exceed input…….emc2 kind of thing? Thanks for any response.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no - you have no need to worry

author: @grendel1960a
text: I bought my first 3d printer 6 years ago, and my second one last year, although the first one I bought was good, it was pretty much hands free, and I didnt have to build it (it was ready to go out of the box- cost was over £600) I can categorically say that in the 5 years between my purchases, 3d printers have gone from difficult tricky machines where everything pretty much had to be spot on to get the result you wanted, to the modern machine that cost me less than £100, and it is by far the better machine, it prints what I want when I want (today I printed a new bracket for the stand on the back of the soft case on my tablet), all in all as a tool its something I would heartily recommend to any tinkerer. in less time than it would take to actually fabricate something, you can set a print going, and get on with another part- like winding the coils, and when you have finished that, there it is a pretty new part, just waiting for you to put together, make a mistake, want to try a new variation, just amend the file, click, off it goes, go to bed and wake up to the part in the morning (or the day after if you are Rob and printing huge things).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - nice one mate - and yes I do print some pretty large things lol

author: @josephpk4878
text: The entire reason I got into the idea of 3D Printing was because of the Darwin wind turbine video, where I had an epiphany on a modification that I wanted to try. After days of research, I ended up buying an Ender 5 S1 and it arrived last week... and it's still sitting in the box, on my workbench. I'm completely intimidated by it, which is ridiculous, because I have lots of experience in mechanics, production, and 3D modelling. I have to laugh at myself, because this video was like an answer to my hesitancy and now I want to pull it out of the box. Once I have it assembled, I'll need another video to encourage me to turn it on.

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: go for it, once you start and see the possibilities, I can guarantee you will be using it all the time, mine gets used at least once a week just for things I need outside of its use for tinkering

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the first steps are always the hardest mate - make them easy for yourself - print something very very simple - just to have success

replies.author: @josephpk4878
replies.text: It's finally out of the box... assembly is tonight's project. I'm glad to have found a method for keeping my mind occupied. I'm currently researching methods to recycle my scrap and am scratching my head over a cheaper solution to produce useable filament - the current solutions are a fortune. I'd feel a lot better about diverting plastics from the garbage dump, so if you're ever pondering the same issue, I'd love to see your take on it. I thank you and your channel for re-inspiring my technical itch.

replies.author: @silverpc4611
replies.text: @josephpk4878  there are some people using recycled plastic bottles to make filament.

author: @vexy1987
text: I was really impressed with my elegoo mars 2 resin printer, however serviceability, whilst thought of by elegoo (they sell spare parts) was let down buy cheap soft screws which rounded off almost immediately, and parts that were glued together which really shouldn't be. A good tool should be easy to maintain and easy to repair, I've had better experiences in that regard with more expensive models from other brands where they don't cut costs so religiously. You get what you pay for I guess!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I haven't had any problems so I can't really comment on serviceability but I will let you know when something comes up for sure

replies.author: @vexy1987
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering no insurmountable issues, just headaches. Wouldn't stop me recommending them.

replies.author: @ET_AYY_LMAO
replies.text:
Resin printers are not for everyone. You need dedicated workspace. It stinks, its a health and environmental hazard. Prints are fragile (but super detailed).
You need to dispose of waste in a safe way, flushing resin is extremely bad for the environment and wildlife.
And yeah the cheap chinese resin printers are build almost like inkjet printers, and they are about as locked in regards to firmware, slicer etc.
